q: Health experts worldwide are concerning about the surge in health problems.
solution: 'Concerning' is an adjective meaning 'worrying', but here we need the past participle 'concerned' as in 'to be concerned about' (= to be worried about). Correct: 'are concerned'.

q: "Embrace change as an opportunity for personal growth and allowed it to improve your life."
solution: The sentence uses parallel imperatives: 'Embrace...and allow...' Both verbs should be in base form (imperative). 'Allowed' must be 'allow'.

q: Kanyakumari is popular for its beautiful beaches, unique Vivekananda Rock Memorial and stunning sunrise and sunset view.
solution: 'Sunrise and sunset view' refers to more than one view (both sunrise AND sunset), so the plural 'views' is required.

q: Mobile phones has become an integral part of life.
solution: 'Mobile phones' is a plural subject. It requires the plural auxiliary verb 'have' — 'Mobile phones have become'.

q: He usually ate breakfast before he goes to school.
solution: The main verb 'ate' is in Simple Past. The subordinate clause must also be in past tense — 'goes' should be 'went' for consistency.

q: Sleep is important in maintain one's health and well being.
solution: 'Important to + infinitive' is the correct structure. 'In maintain' is incorrect; it should be 'important to maintain'.

q: Be the changes you want to see in the world.
solution: The famous quote is 'Be the change you want to see in the world.' 'Change' should be singular — referring to one transformation.

q: The board invite online applications from eligible candidates for the post of a senior English teacher.
solution: 'The board' is a collective singular noun. It takes a singular verb — 'invites' (with 's').

q: Place the garment on the ironing board, smoothening out as many wrinkle as possible.
solution: 'As many' is followed by a countable plural noun — 'wrinkles' (plural), not the singular 'wrinkle'.

q: This product is manufacturing for infants and contains no additives.
solution: The product does not perform manufacturing; it is the object of manufacturing. Passive voice is required — 'is manufactured'.

q: I come across this beautiful painting in an antiques store last week.
solution: 'Last week' is a definite past time marker requiring Simple Past. 'Come' must be 'came'.

q: She may play the piano when she was only eleven.
solution: 'Could' expresses past ability. The sentence refers to her past skill at age eleven — 'could play' is correct, not 'may play'.

q: Air pollution is continuous damaging the whole ecological system affecting the lives of plants and animals as well.
solution: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'damaging'. 'Continuous' is an adjective; the correct adverb form is 'continuously'.

q: This site use cookies. By continuing to use our website, you are agreeing to our privacy policy.
solution: 'This site' is a singular subject. In Simple Present, a singular subject takes a verb with 's' — 'uses'.

q: Our goal is to promoting healthier meal choices in schools and at home.
solution: 'To + base form' (infinitive) is required after 'is to'. 'Promoting' (gerund) is incorrect here — 'is to promote' is correct.

q: We reserve the right to make change to this website at any time without notice.
solution: 'Make changes' is the correct collocation. 'Change' (singular) is incorrect when referring to multiple possible amendments.

q: The soup recipe didn't turned out as expected, it was lacking in flavour.
solution: After the auxiliary 'did not/didn't', the base form of the verb is used — 'didn't turn' (not 'didn't turned').

q: Our latest software solution is designed for help businesses streamline their operations.
solution: 'Designed to + infinitive' is the correct structure for expressing purpose. 'For help' is incorrect — it should be 'to help'.

q: There's a significant correlation of low income and high crime rates.
solution: 'Correlation' always takes the preposition 'between' (not 'of') — 'correlation between A and B' is the correct fixed expression.

q: Some features cannot be available on your computer.
solution: 'Cannot be available' is contradictory — a feature either is or isn't available. 'May not be available' correctly expresses possibility.

q: When marginalized voices get a chance to tell their story, anger or sadness would be a justified theme.
solution: The sentence makes a general statement about what happens when marginalized voices speak — Simple Future 'will be' is more appropriate than 'would be' for a general truth.

q: "Where words fall music speak." Each batch will contain only five students which will assure personal attention.
solution: 'Music' is a singular subject. Simple Present requires 's' — 'music speaks'.

q: One of the world's most popular domain name seller, provides fantastic web hosting packages as well.
solution: 'One of the most popular sellers' — 'one of' is followed by a plural noun. 'Seller' must be 'sellers'.

q: The upma recipe was so delicious that I couldn't barely stop myself from having a second helping.
solution: Double negatives are incorrect. 'Couldn't barely' uses two negatives ('couldn't' + 'barely'). The correct form is 'could barely' (single negative meaning).

q: Nearly all the sports practiced nowadays is competitive.
solution: 'All the sports' is a plural subject. It requires the plural verb 'are' — 'all the sports...are competitive'.

q: Honesty not only succeed in the long run but also brings about mutual trust and confidence.
solution: 'Honesty' is a singular uncountable noun. Simple Present requires 's' on the verb — 'succeeds'.

q: Both the boys have got a scolding but either of them paid heed.
solution: The sentence implies that none of the boys paid heed (negative meaning). 'Either' (used for one of two) should be 'neither' (not one of the two).

q: World leaders should work to establish peace of the world.
solution: The correct phrase is 'peace in the world' — the preposition 'in' indicates location. 'Peace of the world' is incorrect.

q: A story, to engage the interest of the read, must include mystery and adventure.
solution: 'Read' is a verb, but a noun is needed here — 'the reader' (the person reading). 'Interest of the reader' is correct.

q: The winning athletes were felicitation by the State Government and the City Council.
solution: 'Were felicitated' requires the past participle of the verb 'felicitate' (= to congratulate formally). 'Felicitation' is a noun, not a verb form — incorrect here.

q: There was exciting in the air when the winning school team marched into the hall.
solution: 'Excitement' is the noun needed after 'was'. 'Exciting' is an adjective/verb form — 'there was excitement in the air'.

q: The art displays in the wall were a wonderful way for children to express their talent.
solution: Art is displayed 'on' a wall (surface), not 'in' a wall. 'On the wall' is the correct prepositional phrase.

q: My sister lives at the ground floor of a two-storeyed apartment.
solution: Floors of a building use the preposition 'on' — 'lives on the ground floor'. 'At' is incorrect.

q: I can't never forget my childhood friend Bella.
solution: 'Can't never' is a double negative (two negative words together), which is grammatically incorrect in standard English. 'Can never' is correct.

q: Playing outdoor games have benefits over being indoors.
solution: 'Playing outdoor games' is a gerund phrase acting as a singular subject. It requires the singular verb 'has'.

q: The sounds of laughter was echoing in the corridor.
solution: The subject is 'sounds' (plural). The verb must agree — 'were echoing' (plural past continuous).

q: The children were exciting when they saw the leopard.
solution: 'Excited' (past participle) describes how the children felt. 'Exciting' means something that causes excitement in others — 'the children were excited' is correct.

q: Water was vital to human health and fitness.
solution: Water's importance to health is a permanent/universal truth. Simple Present 'is' is used for general truths — 'water is vital'.

q: Spending time for their kids brings immediate as well as long-last gain for a parents.
solution: 'Long-lasting' is the correct compound adjective meaning 'enduring over a long period'. 'Long-last' is not a standard English word.

q: The city police have decide to taking stern action against drivers which attempt to overtake in the left side on city roads.
solution: 'Have' is a Present Perfect auxiliary requiring the past participle 'decided' — 'have decided'. 'Decide' is the base form and incorrect.

q: Airports have became an important part of big city now. Small planes are being use by some people to travelling from one city to another.
solution: 'Have + past participle' requires 'become' (not 'became' which is Simple Past). 'Have become' is the correct Present Perfect form.

q: The pleasure in being outdoors is fundamental for human happiness. There are so much to learn.
solution: 'There is so much to learn' — 'much' (uncountable) requires the singular 'is', not 'are'. 'There are so many things' would be an alternative phrasing but is not the error here.

q: It is an constant source of delight to know more on the flora and fauna around.
solution: 'Know about' is the correct phrasal verb — 'know about flora and fauna'. 'Know on' is not a standard English expression.

q: Countries near on equator are much warmer then countries farthest to the north and south.
solution: 'Than' is used in comparisons — 'warmer than'. 'Then' refers to time (sequence of events) and is incorrect in a comparison.

q: In the prisoner's room a candle is burning dimly. Only him back, the hair by his head, and his hands are visible from outside.
solution: A possessive adjective is needed before the noun 'back'. 'Him' is an object pronoun — 'his back' is correct.

q: On the way back, one of his men fall into a coma.
solution: The narrative is in past tense. 'Fall' (present) must be 'fell' (Simple Past) to maintain tense consistency.

q: Scott is left with none other alternative but to pitch his tent and waiting for the blizzard to pass.
solution: 'But to + base verb' is a fixed structure — 'but to pitch and wait'. 'Waiting' (gerund/continuous) is grammatically incorrect in parallel with 'to pitch'.

q: Unfortunately the blizzard grown fiercer.
solution: Simple Past 'grew' is needed for a narrative past event. 'Grown' is a past participle (requires auxiliary 'had' — 'had grown').

q: It can happen only at India.
solution: Countries use the preposition 'in' — 'in India'. 'At' is used for specific points/locations (bus stop, airport etc.), not countries.

q: We, here in India have so many religions, we ate so many different foods.
solution: The sentence describes an ongoing cultural reality, not a past event. Simple Present 'eat' is correct — 'we eat many different foods'.

q: We wear so much different kinds of dresses. Still we are much united than any other country on the world.
solution: 'Kinds' is a countable plural noun, requiring 'many' (not 'much' which is for uncountable nouns) — 'so many different kinds'.

q: We should been proud of ourselves. Each one of us have good qualities.
solution: 'Should + base verb' is the correct modal structure. 'Been' is a past participle — 'should be' (not 'should been') is correct.

q: In this way we can hoped to succeed in life.
solution: 'Can + base verb' is the modal structure. 'Hoped' is past tense — 'can hope' (base form) is correct.

q: I met her by chance then I went to getting some medicine which Sister Amy use to give.
solution: 'Used to' expresses a past habitual action. 'Use to' (without 'd') is incorrect — 'used to give' is the standard form.

q: It was amazing for meet Mother. I start loving just her presence.
solution: 'Amazing to + infinitive' is the correct structure for purpose/experience. 'For meet' is incorrect — 'amazing to meet' is right.

q: Being with her give one a new kind on energy.
solution: 'A kind of energy' is the correct prepositional phrase. 'Kind on' is not a standard expression — 'kind of' is correct.

q: My meetings of Mother had always been a finer experience. I would go to him whenever I was sad, happy and angry.
solution: Mother is a female subject. The pronoun must be 'her' — 'I would go to her' (not 'him').

q: She said that whenever I go to parties, if here was any leftover food I should picked it up.
solution: 'Here' refers to a location near the speaker. In reported speech/narrative, 'there' is used for a location away from the speaker — 'if there was any leftover food'.

q: Bring it on the poor people. It was one of a most difficult things to do.
solution: Superlatives use the definite article 'the' — 'one of the most difficult things'. 'A most' is incorrect for a superlative structure.

q: It is well know that in winter birds fly in the sunny South, or in spring, they fly back North to their breed grounds they had leave behind.
solution: 'Well known' is the correct compound adjective (past participle). 'Know' is the base verb form — 'well known' is correct.

q: Research is an detailed study of a subject undertaking on a systematic basis in order to increase a stock of knowledge.
solution: 'A' is used before consonant sounds. 'Detailed' begins with the consonant sound /d/ — 'a detailed study' is correct, not 'an'.
